title: Chapter 26: Tea and Lettuce
content: Three o'clock.

Not three o'clock the way most people mean it—a rough estimate, a casual glance at the phone, a somewhere-around-three. Three o'clock the way Mrs. Hendricks meant it: precisely, reliably, as if the universe had been constructed around this single daily appointment.
At 2:55 PM, she opened her apartment door.
Not all the way. Just a crack, then a few inches more, then enough for the hallway light to spill in and for a 150-pound capybara to squeeze through if she happened to be passing by.

Mrs. Hendricks was seventy-two. Her hair was white and cut practical, and her hands had the particular look of hands that used to do things—knit, garden, roll pie crust—and now mostly waited. She wore house slippers every day. Not because she couldn't afford shoes. Because she had nowhere to go that required different ones.
At 2:57 PM, she put the plate on the floor just inside the doorway.

Romaine lettuce, sliced thin. One apple, cored and cut into pieces small enough for a mouth that was mostly designed for grass. A single baby carrot, because Mrs. Hendricks had read somewhere that capybaras liked carrots and refused to fact-check it because the article had also said they were "gentle souls" and she liked that part.
At 3:00 PM exactly, Chloe appeared.

Not every day. Most days. Enough that Mrs. Hendricks had stopped being surprised and started being relieved, which is a different thing entirely. Surprise is for things that might not happen. Relief is for things that you need to happen, and then do.

Chloe walked through the open door, slow and ambling, the way she walked everywhere because capybaras did not rush and because, Chloe had discovered, rushing was overrated and probably the reason she'd had stress migraines for ten consecutive years of human life.

Mrs. Hendricks lowered herself into the folding chair by the door. Not a nice chair. A folding chair, the kind you buy at a hardware store when you need something to sit on and can't imagine caring enough to want more. She'd had it since before Chloe. Since before the ESA letter. Since before the quiet settled into her apartment like a guest who refused to leave and then stopped being a guest and started being the house itself.
"Hello, dear," Mrs. Hendricks said.

This was how it started every time. Hello, dear. As if Chloe were a neighbor stopping by for tea. As if this were normal, which—it was, actually. Chloe had come to understand that normal was just whatever you did long enough that it stopped surprising you.

Chloe ate the lettuce first, because romaine was crisp and good and she had opinions about salad now, which was weird but also maybe the most reliably content she'd ever been about anything. The apple pieces next. The baby carrot last, and only because Mrs. Hendricks was watching with the particular hope of someone who wanted her offering to be accepted.
*Carrots are fine,* Chloe thought, chewing. *Not great. Fine. The apple is better. But you got me the carrot because you read that I'd like it, and that matters more than the carrot itself.*
She couldn't say any of that, obviously. So she ate the carrot with what she hoped was gratitude, and then she settled onto the floor beside Mrs. Hendricks' chair.
They sat.

This was the whole appointment, and it was enough. Two creatures in an open doorway, one in a folding chair and one on the linoleum, sharing nothing but the fact that they were both here. The hallway was quiet. The apartment building made its building sounds—a pipe groaning somewhere, the distant murmur of a television through a wall, the particular hum of a structure that held dozens of separate lives and none of them talked to each other.
Mrs. Hendricks talked, though. Not every day. Most days. Enough.

"My husband's name was Richard," she said today, because today was a talking day, and you could tell those by the way she looked at the empty space in the corner where a chair used to be. "I don't think I've told you that. Richard Hendricks. Forty-six years."

Chloe listened. She was very good at listening, partly because capybaras are naturally attentive and partly because she used to be bad at it as a human—always formulating her response, always planning the next thing to say—and the universe had apparently decided to teach her by taking her words away entirely.

"He was a quiet man." Mrs. Hendricks folded her hands in her lap. "Not the fun kind of quiet. The other kind. The kind where you come home from somewhere and he's sitting in his chair and you say, 'How was your day?' and he says, 'Fine,' and that's it. That's the whole conversation. Fine."
Her voice didn't crack. It did something worse—it went perfectly level, like someone who had practiced keeping it level for so long that the flatness had become its own kind of wound.

"I used to think—I used to think if I just said the right thing, or made the right dinner, or—" She stopped. Shook her head. "No. That's not fair. He wasn't cruel. He was just... quiet. And after a while, I was quiet too. You know how that happens? You stop talking because there's no one talking back, and then you forget that you ever had things to say."
Chloe did know. She knew the way silence could calcify around a person, like sediment, layer on layer, until you couldn't tell the difference between choosing not to speak and being unable to.

She shifted her weight and leaned her head against Mrs. Hendricks' foot. Slowly, carefully, the way she did everything—the way you touch a person you don't want to startle, a person you want to know you're there without making it A Whole Thing.
Mrs. Hendricks' hand came down, automatically, and rested on Chloe's head. Her fingers worked gently through the coarse fur.

"I got the letter because my doctor said I needed something to care for," Mrs. Hendricks said. Her voice had gone softer. Not quieter—softer. The difference between someone lowering their voice and someone removing the armor from it. "He said—Dr. Pham, he's a good man—he said, 'Helen, you need a reason to get up in the morning.' And I said, 'I get up every morning,' and he said, 'But do you have a reason?'"
She was quiet for a moment. Her fingers paused in Chloe's fur.

"I couldn't even take care of a plant. You know that? My daughter—you haven't met my daughter, she's in Portland—I told her I was thinking about a cat, and she said, 'Mom, you forgot to water the Christmas cactus three years running.' Which was true. I did forget. I forgot because there was no one to notice whether I forgot or not, and caring about something when no one is watching is—it's harder than it sounds."
Chloe pressed her head a little more firmly against Mrs. Hendricks' foot. *I'm watching,* she wanted to say. *I notice.*
"But then you showed up in the hallway."
Mrs. Hendricks laughed. It was a small laugh, barely a sound, but it existed, which meant it was real.

"Just—walked in. Like you owned the place. Do you remember? It was raining. You were wet. And you walked past three other apartments and sat right outside my door, and I opened it, and you looked at me like—"
Her voice cracked. First time. The sediment split.
"—like I was worth sitting with."

Chloe didn't move. She held perfectly, impossibly still—the kind of stillness that capybaras are famous for, the kind that looks like laziness but is actually the hardest thing in the world: being present without trying to fix anything.
Mrs. Hendricks' hand was trembling on Chloe's head. Just slightly. A seventy-two-year-old tremor that she probably thought nobody noticed.
"You looked at me like there was nothing wrong with me," Mrs. Hendricks whispered. "Like I was already enough. Just sitting there."
She was crying.

Not sobbing—nothing dramatic, nothing that would carry through the thin walls to the neighbors. Just tears, sliding down the kind of face that had learned to cry quietly because Richard was sleeping, or watching TV, or sitting in his chair, and she hadn't wanted to bother him with the inconvenience of her sadness.
Chloe didn't move.

She didn't nuzzle or lick or do any of the things that a pet would do to comfort an owner. She wasn't a pet. She was a person who happened to currently be a capybara, and she knew—she knew in her bones, in the place where her human instincts still lived—that sometimes the only thing to do with someone's pain is to sit next to it and let it exist.

Mrs. Hendricks cried for maybe ninety seconds. It felt longer. It felt like years. It felt like all the quiet days of a long marriage that was quiet when it shouldn't have been, and empty before it needed to be, and gone now in a way that left a chair-shaped space in the corner that she couldn't bring herself to fill or to move.
Then she stopped. Wiped her face with the back of her hand. Sniffed once, decisively, in the way of women who have finished crying and want the room to know it.
"She's my friend," Mrs. Hendricks said.

This was not to Chloe. This was to a neighbor—Mr. Podolski from 4B—who had paused in the hallway, grocery bag in hand, staring at the scene: an old woman crying in a folding chair with a capybara at her feet.
He stared. Then he nodded, confused but not unkind, and walked on.

Mrs. Hendricks meant it, though. *She's my friend.* The way only lonely people can mean things—as if the words are fragile and enormous and true, and saying them out loud is the bravest thing you can do because it means admitting you needed them.
Chloe hummed. Low and long. The most comforting sound she knew how to make—the sound of *I'm here, I'm not leaving, you don't have to be okay for me.*

Mrs. Hendricks' hand settled back into her fur, and they stayed like that until the lettuce plate was empty and the light coming through the hallway window had gone gold, and the day felt like it had been worth getting up for after all.

That night, after Chloe had padded back to Maya's apartment and the door was closed and the building was settling into its nighttime quiet, Mrs. Hendricks stood in her living room and looked at the corner.
The corner where Richard's chair had been.

She'd left the space empty for—how long? A year? Eighteen months? The chair itself was in the back of the closet, covered with a dry-cleaning bag, because she couldn't throw it away and couldn't sit in it and couldn't look at it, so she'd hidden it like a wound she wasn't ready to heal.
She stood there for a long time.
Then she walked to the closet. Opened it. Pushed aside winter coats and a box of ornaments she never used. Gripped the dry-cleaning bag and pulled.

The chair slid out. It was a brown armchair, worn at the arms, slightly tilted to the left from decades of a man sitting in it and leaning in the same direction. It smelled like dust and old fabric and something underneath that might have been Richard's aftershave or might have been memory, which smelled the same.
Mrs. Hendricks carried it to the corner. Set it down. It fit in the empty space like it had never left.
She looked at it.
Then she sat in it.

The cushion sighed under her weight—different weight than it was used to, lighter, more careful. The armrest was still worn smooth on the right side where Richard's hand had rested for forty-six years. She ran her fingers over the spot.

She reached for the remote. Not the remote Richard had always kept on the armrest—she'd put that in a drawer months ago. Her remote. The one she'd bought herself, with bigger buttons because her eyes weren't what they used to be.
She turned on the TV.

Not for noise. She'd been using the TV for noise for a year—turning it on in the morning, leaving it running all day, letting the sound of other people's voices fill the rooms so she could pretend she wasn't alone.

She turned it on because she wanted to. Because there was a documentary about birds on PBS that she'd been wanting to watch, and she hadn't watched it because watching something you enjoy alone is sadder than not watching it at all, somehow. Like admitting that the best part of your day is something no one will ever share with you.
But today—because of the lettuce, and the apple, and the baby carrot that a capybara ate with what might have been gratitude—today she wanted to.
So she did.
Mrs. Hendricks sat in her dead husband's chair and watched a documentary about birds and did not feel alone. The apartment was still quiet. The walls were still thin. Richard was still gone.

But there was a plate by the door with romaine residue on it, and tomorrow at three o'clock, she would open the door again, and a 150-pound capybara would walk through it and eat her lettuce and sit beside her chair, and that was— That was enough. That was more than enough.
That was everything, actually.

On the screen, a blue heron took flight. Mrs. Hendricks watched it rise and thought about all the things that lift off when we let them—silence, and grief, and the strange, stubborn belief that we are not worth sitting with.
She fell asleep in the chair at nine-thirty.
The TV played softly until two in the morning, when it turned itself off automatically, and the apartment was quiet again.
But it was a different quiet now.
It was the quiet of a place where someone had decided to stay.
